title: Dog Chewing Paws Until Raw: Causes, Urgency, and Care Tips
meta_desc: Is your dog chewing their paws raw? Learn about common causes, when to see a vet, and how a photo helps with immediate triage and treatment.
content_html:
What is it?

When a dog chews their paws until the skin becomes raw, it is usually a manifestation of intense itching or localized pain. This behavior is often linked to allergic reactions (environmental or food-based), parasitic infestations like mites or fleas, or even psychological triggers such as boredom or separation anxiety. As the dog continues to lick and chew, they damage the skin barrier, which frequently leads to secondary bacterial or yeast infections that cause even more irritation, creating a painful cycle.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While paw chewing is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it indicates that your pet is in significant distress and discomfort. You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours to prevent the development of a deep tissue infection (cellulitis). However, if you notice the paw is severely swollen, oozing pus, or if your dog has a fever and is refusing to eat, you should seek veterinary care as soon as possible.

Why a photo helps triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photo of the raw areas helps a veterinary professional perform an initial assessment of the severity. A photo allows the triage team to see if there is visible swelling, discharge, or foreign objects like thorns or grass seeds stuck between the toes. It also serves as a baseline to track whether the inflammation is spreading or improving once treatment begins. Capturing an image before your dog licks the area further can provide a much clearer picture of the primary skin lesion.

faqs.question: Can I put human antiseptic on my dog's raw paws?
faqs.answer: You should avoid using human medications or ointments without a vet's approval, as many contain ingredients that are toxic if ingested when the dog licks their paws.

faqs.question: Should I use a cone or E-collar?
faqs.answer: Yes, using an Elizabethan collar (cone) is highly recommended to prevent further self-trauma and allow the raw skin a chance to start healing before your vet appointment.

faqs.question: How can I soothe the paws at home?
faqs.answer: A cool water soak or a clean, damp cloth can provide temporary relief from itching, but ensure the paws are dried thoroughly afterward, as moisture can encourage yeast growth.
